description: RDD with single bandwidth, no sensitivity analysis, and manipulation concerns
input_text:
2. Empirical Strategy

We exploit a discontinuity in China's college entrance exam (gaokao) scoring system. Students scoring above the province-specific "first-tier" (一本线) cutoff gain admission to elite universities, while those just below attend second-tier institutions. This creates a sharp discontinuity in educational quality at the cutoff.

We estimate the effect of elite university attendance on starting salary using a local linear regression:

Salary_i = α + β * EliteUniv_i + f(Score_i - Cutoff) + ε_i

where f(·) is a local linear function estimated within a bandwidth of h = 30 points around the cutoff. EliteUniv_i is instrumented by the indicator 1(Score_i ≥ Cutoff).

3. Results

The RDD estimate indicates that elite university attendance increases starting salary by 23% (β = 0.23, SE = 0.08, p < 0.01). The effect is estimated using 4,200 observations within our 30-point bandwidth.

Figure 3 shows the jump in elite university enrollment probability at the cutoff, confirming a strong first stage (jump = 0.72).

4. Robustness

As a robustness check, we add covariates (gender, urban hukou, parental income). The estimate remains similar at β = 0.21 (SE = 0.09).
rationale: Three critical RDD-specific issues: (1) Only ONE bandwidth (h=30) is reported with no sensitivity analysis. Best practice (Imbens & Kalyanaraman 2012, Calonico et al. 2014) requires showing results across multiple bandwidths and reporting the MSE-optimal bandwidth. (2) No McCrary density test or manipulation check — gaokao scores near cutoffs are highly strategic (students retake, scores may be manipulated for border cases), making a density test essential. (3) No placebo cutoff tests to verify the discontinuity is specific to the actual threshold. A single-bandwidth RDD without these checks would not survive peer review at any serious applied micro journal.
